---
title: "The Living Data Project: Data Rescue Guidebook"
---

# Welcome

This guidebook supports graduate student data rescue teams and interns participating in the Living Data Project. The Living Data Project is a Canada-wide initiative to preserve and breathe new life into legacy datasets in ecology, evolution and environmental science, and to train graduate students in best practices in data management, reproducible research, synthesis statistics, and scientific collaboration.

# Authorship

This is a living document. The first draft was written by Luis Francisco Henao Diaz, Freek de Haas and Diane S. Srivastava, but it has subsequently been improved by many members of the Living Data Project including: Dominique Roche, Kerri Finlay, Sally Taylor, and Bryan Currinder.

# Goals

After working through and referring back to this guide, individuals should be better equipped to:

-   Assess legacy datasets
-   Create high-quality metadata
-   Apply reproducible workflows
-   Prepare datasets for data repositories
-   Practice responsible data stewardship

<br>

# How to use this guide

## Navigation and Organization

This guidebook is organized to follow the progression of a typical data rescue project while also introducing the broader principles that underpin responsible data stewardship. The early chapters establish the conceptual foundation for data rescue, including the importance of metadata, open science, reproducibility, and long-term data preservation. These concepts provide the context for understanding not only how to rescue data, but why these practices matter.

Building on this foundation, the guide then walks through the major stages of a data rescue project---from preparing for a project and working with data owners, through data cleaning, standardization, documentation, quality assurance, and final publication. While these chapters are presented in a logical sequence, data rescue is often an iterative process. You may find yourself revisiting earlier chapters as new questions arise or as your understanding of a dataset evolves.

Throughout the guide, you'll also encounter content that is specific to particular repositories or Living Data Project activities (e.g. callout sections relevant to individuals working with DataStream). These sections build on the general principles presented earlier and demonstrate how those principles are implemented within specific workflows. This approach is intended to help you develop skills that extend beyond any single repository or project and can be applied across a wide range of data rescue efforts.

## Learning and reference

This guide is intended to be more than a manual. Throughout the guide, you'll find examples, templates, checklists, reflection questions, and practical exercises designed to help you actively work through each stage of a data rescue project. As your project progresses, you can return to relevant chapters whenever you need guidance, best practices, or compiled resources.
